item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"A small percentage subjects"

I think you want an "of" in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, the cult has been eliminated. This feels stronger as some kind of folk or hippy medicinal thing, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree! Let me try to punch that up a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of something that allows you to see horrible things that go unnoticed by the rest of humanity, on its own, has been done to the point where I believe something else of interest beyond "well I guess that's a thing" is necessary before it's a workable article. The fact that it's unnoticeable surgery is the start of something interesting, I suppose.

I really didn't care for the "and it was a spooooky cult" explanation, which isn't elucidated at all beyond the fact that it exists and that it made this stuff. I'm not saying that I want to read reams of reports on a goofy cult, because I don't think I do, but the offhanded nature of the reference makes it feel like a "eh, a wizard did it or something" handwaved explanation.

In some ways, I think no explanation at all would be preferable. The fact that any old goober can put some household chemicals together and suddenly be aware of this bizarre phenomenon is the basis of something interesting; making it the product of the Foundationverse's apparently-thriving Spooky Doomsday Cult Community only cheapens the horror, I feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an improvement. Moved into no vote territory. I'll reread on a later date. If I like it then, I'll upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I've tried to punch it up a bit, hint more as to what is happening, and create a stronger narrative both in the cult portion (trying to attract the attention of the phantom surgeons) and the Foundation angle (this SCP was quickly written off as fairly harmless and thus has fallen through the cracks despite having serious implications, playing on the increasingly massive list of ever-more worldthreatening and intensively wrangled SCPs we have on the list).

This one is a bit of a balancing act. If I hint in the document too strongly that something is up, it becomes unbelievable that the Foundation would like this slip by. If it is too subtle, it just becomes crazypaint. I hope this is a more workable balance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another suggestion: maybe make it so that this stuff sometimes just appears on random people in the areas that it seems like the cuts are being made, where our invisible surgeons make contact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If there was an emphasis on brevity, its reversed itself. A short article has to do something really impressive these days to stick around. For me this manages to do that, but I'd love to see it explored a bit more thoroughly.

On a related note, overt cross references tend to be torches-and-pitchforks territory these days unless they are very well done, but I can't help but think of SCP-415…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The imagery is great but there's nothing more in terms of story or emotional investment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was actually aiming for the implication that people were being experimented on without their knowledge; proposed cuts drawn and erased and redrawn over and over before the incision is finally made, mysterious things poking and prodding at you in the night and leaving evidence only visible by a substance dismissed as a mere hallucinogen by the Foundation.

Now see, if you hinted at this more clearly, it would have a greater impact. Maybe have vague shadowy things as part of the hallucination? Is that too cliché? It's probably too cliché.

Elaboration on the cult that invented it might help. Say, transcripts of one of their prayers/chants or an interview log with a cultist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you check out a few random Series 3 SCPs, you will see that they are usually much longer than this. I have no problems with this being so short, but I don't find it interesting enough to upvote.
Censorship in the Procedures section should be avoided as it is the necessary info required for the containment. How would the researchers know when to inspect again if you censor the date?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One aspect that made me curious is the mention that it is easy to produce the SCP from household chemicals. My question is: How easy would it be to make it? Could someone accidentally make it without knowing what he/she is doing? Would there be the need for drastic containment measures since it could be made easily? That is an interesting angle to look at it.

Also, how would that tie in to the cult that kept a sample of the SCP? Why do they have a supply of it? What do they intend to do with it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… really?

Because when I was active in 2012 or so, there was a great emphasis on brevity. I actually was worried this article was too long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral/no vote because I love this concept but I feel like it's too short of an article. I want to know more about the cult that was doing this stuff, or any sort of backstory really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooh, that's an interesting interpretation I hadn't actually been going for!

I was actually aiming for the implication that people were being experimented on without their knowledge; proposed cuts drawn and erased and redrawn over and over before the incision is finally made, mysterious things poking and prodding at you in the night and leaving evidence only visible by a substance dismissed as a mere hallucinogen by the Foundation. It was inspired by me waking up in a cold sweat this morning having just had a nightmare about being dissected which freaked me the fuck out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhh. It's creepy imagery, but that's all it is. I mean, yes, seeing scars from one's own autopsy would be terrifying, but this isn't effective at eliciting that horror. I don't know what to say besides that, sorry.
